era_context: The late 2010s were defined by rising nationalist populism, accelerating great-power competition between the United States and China, the ongoing digital transformation of economies and societies, and deepening climate anxiety. The post-2008 global economic recovery continued unevenly, with growing inequality fuelling political polarisation across democracies. The Syrian civil war entered its seventh year, producing massive refugee flows that continued to reshape European and Middle Eastern politics. China under Xi Jinping consolidated power domestically while expanding influence through the Belt and Road Initiative.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (limited English-language coverage of domestic political developments in Turkmenistan, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan)
Pacific Island nations (climate adaptation efforts and political developments underreported)
Central Africa (ongoing conflicts in CAR, DRC received less global media attention relative to their severity)

events.title: Inter-Korean diplomatic thaw and summits
events.description: A dramatic diplomatic opening between North and South Korea unfolded in 2018, catalysed by North Korea's participation in the Pyeongchang Winter Olympics in February. South Korean President Moon Jae-in and North Korean leader Kim Jong-un held three summit meetings — on 27 April at Panmunjom, 26 May at Panmunjom again, and 18–20 September in Pyongyang. The April summit produced the Panmunjom Declaration, pledging denuclearisation and a formal end to the Korean War. While these summits generated enormous optimism, concrete denuclearisation steps did not materialise, and the diplomatic momentum would stall by 2019.
key_figures: Kim Jong-un (Supreme Leader of North Korea)
Kim Yo-jong (Vice Director, Propaganda and Agitation Department, DPRK)

sources.name: Panmunjom Declaration for Peace, Prosperity and Reunification of the Korean Peninsula (27 April 2018)
Reuters, AP, Yonhap News Agency contemporary reporting

events.certainty_note: Heavily documented with live global media coverage, official texts, and extensive photographic/video evidence.

events.title: Trump–Kim Singapore Summit
events.description: On 12 June 2018, US President Donald Trump and North Korean leader Kim Jong-un met in Singapore for the first-ever summit between a sitting US president and a North Korean leader. The meeting produced a joint statement with vague commitments to denuclearisation of the Korean Peninsula, security guarantees, and new US-DPRK relations. Trump also announced a suspension of US-South Korean joint military exercises, surprising both Pentagon officials and Seoul. The summit was widely seen as a historic diplomatic spectacle but produced no verifiable denuclearisation framework, and follow-up negotiations in subsequent months stalled.
key_figures: Donald Trump (President of the United States)
Kim Jong-un (Supreme Leader of North Korea)

sources.name: Joint Statement of President Donald J. Trump and Chairman Kim Jong Un at the Singapore Summit (12 June 2018)
Contemporary reporting by international press corps (AP, Reuters, BBC, NYT, Washington Post)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented global media event with official statements and extensive video/photographic evidence.

events.title: US-China trade war escalation
events.description: In 2018, the United States and China entered a rapidly escalating trade conflict. Beginning in March, the Trump administration imposed tariffs on steel and aluminium imports, followed in July by 25% tariffs on $34 billion of Chinese goods, expanding to $250 billion by September. China retaliated with tariffs on US agricultural products, automobiles, and energy. The conflict was framed by the US as addressing intellectual property theft, forced technology transfer, and trade imbalances, while China characterised it as economic bullying. The trade war disrupted global supply chains, depressed agricultural commodity prices, and introduced significant uncertainty into the global economy.
key_figures: Donald Trump (President of the United States)

sources.name: US Federal Register: Presidential Proclamations and Executive Orders on tariffs (2018)
Office of the United States Trade Representative, Section 301 Investigation findings (March 2018)
IMF World Economic Outlook, October 2018

events.certainty_note: Documented through official government proclamations, trade data, and extensive economic analysis.

events.title: Murder of Jamal Khashoggi
events.description: On 2 October 2018, Saudi journalist and Washington Post columnist Jamal Khashoggi was killed inside the Saudi consulate in Istanbul, Turkey, by a team of Saudi operatives. Turkish intelligence released evidence including audio recordings, and the killing provoked a major international crisis. The CIA assessed with high confidence that Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman had ordered the operation, though Saudi Arabia denied his involvement. The murder damaged Saudi Arabia's international reputation, temporarily strained US-Saudi relations, and drew global attention to press freedom and Saudi Arabia's authoritarian governance, though it ultimately produced limited lasting policy consequences.
key_figures: Mohammed bin Salman (Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia)

sources.name: United Nations Special Rapporteur report by Agnès Callamard (June 2019, investigating 2018 events)
Turkish government-released intelligence and audio evidence (October–November 2018)
CIA assessment reported by Washington Post and Wall Street Journal (November 2018)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through intelligence evidence, forensic investigation, and international inquiry. The role of MBS remains officially disputed by Saudi Arabia but is accepted by most independent assessments.

events.title: Xi Jinping abolishes presidential term limits
events.description: In March 2018, China's National People's Congress voted to remove the two-term limit on the presidency from the constitution, enabling Xi Jinping to remain in power indefinitely. The amendment passed with 2,958 votes in favour, 2 against, and 3 abstentions. This was the most significant change to China's political succession system since Deng Xiaoping introduced term limits in 1982 specifically to prevent the return of Mao-era strongman rule. The move consolidated Xi's personal authority beyond any Chinese leader since Mao and was widely interpreted as marking the end of China's post-Mao collective leadership model.
key_figures: Xi Jinping (President of China, General Secretary of the CPC)

sources.name: Amendment to the Constitution of the People's Republic of China, adopted 11 March 2018 by the 13th NPC
Xinhua News Agency official reporting; international coverage by BBC, NYT, Reuters

events.certainty_note: Official constitutional amendment with public vote tally.

events.title: Ethiopian political transformation under Abiy Ahmed
events.description: Abiy Ahmed became Prime Minister of Ethiopia on 2 April 2018 following the resignation of Hailemariam Desalegn amid years of anti-government protests, particularly by the Oromo and Amhara communities. Abiy rapidly implemented sweeping reforms: releasing thousands of political prisoners, lifting the state of emergency, unblocking websites, legalising opposition groups, welcoming exiled dissidents, and appointing women to half of ministerial positions. Most dramatically, he signed a peace agreement with Eritrea in July, ending a 20-year military standoff. These reforms earned him the 2019 Nobel Peace Prize, though ethnic tensions and political violence continued to intensify, foreshadowing the Tigray War of 2020.
key_figures: Hailemariam Desalegn (outgoing Prime Minister of Ethiopia)

sources.name: Joint Declaration of Peace and Friendship between Eritrea and Ethiopia (9 July 2018)
International Crisis Group reporting on Ethiopian reforms (2018)
Reuters, AP, BBC Amharic Service contemporary coverage

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented through official announcements, international media, and diplomatic records.

events.title: Brazil elects Jair Bolsonaro
events.description: Far-right former military officer Jair Bolsonaro won Brazil's presidential election on 28 October 2018 with 55% of the vote, defeating Workers' Party candidate Fernando Haddad. Bolsonaro's campaign capitalised on widespread disillusionment with the political establishment following the Lava Jato corruption scandal, rising crime, and economic stagnation. His victory marked a sharp rightward turn for Latin America's largest democracy. Bolsonaro's platform included promises of deregulation, relaxed environmental enforcement in the Amazon, and conservative social policies. His presidency would be marked by controversial Amazon deforestation policies and a contentious COVID-19 response.
key_figures: Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva (imprisoned former President, barred from running)

sources.name: Tribunal Superior Eleitoral (Brazilian Superior Electoral Court) official results
Folha de S.Paulo, O Globo, Estadão, Reuters, AP contemporary coverage

events.certainty_note: Official election results with full documentation.

events.title: IPCC Special Report on Global Warming of 1.5°C
events.description: In October 2018, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change released its Special Report on the impacts of global warming of 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. The report concluded that limiting warming to 1.5°C rather than 2°C would significantly reduce risks of extreme weather, sea-level rise, Arctic ice loss, and biodiversity decline. It stated that achieving this target required 'rapid, far-reaching and unprecedented changes in all aspects of society,' including reducing global CO₂ emissions by 45% from 2010 levels by 2030 and reaching net zero by 2050. The report galvanised climate activism, notably influencing the school strike movement led by Greta Thunberg.
key_figures: Valérie Masson-Delmotte (Co-chair, IPCC Working Group I)
Greta Thunberg (climate activist, began school strikes August 2018)

sources.name: IPCC, 2018: Global Warming of 1.5°C. Special Report (SR15)
Nature, Science contemporary analysis and commentary

events.certainty_note: Official IPCC publication with peer-reviewed underlying research.

events.title: Cambridge Analytica / Facebook data scandal
events.description: In March 2018, The Guardian, The New York Times, and Channel 4 News revealed that Cambridge Analytica, a British political consulting firm, had harvested personal data from up to 87 million Facebook users without their consent through a personality quiz app. The data was used for targeted political advertising, including work for the Trump 2016 campaign and the Brexit Leave campaign. Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg testified before the US Congress in April. The scandal triggered a global reckoning over data privacy, platform power, and election integrity, contributing to regulatory momentum behind the EU's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which took effect in May 2018.

sources.name: The Guardian, The New York Times, Channel 4 News investigative reporting (March 2018)
US Senate and House committee hearing transcripts, Zuckerberg testimony (April 2018)
UK Information Commissioner's Office investigation report

events.certainty_note: Documented through whistleblower testimony, leaked documents, official investigations, and congressional hearings.

events.title: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) takes effect
events.description: The EU's General Data Protection Regulation came into force on 25 May 2018, after a two-year implementation period following its 2016 adoption. GDPR established comprehensive data protection rules for all individuals within the EU and the European Economic Area, including requirements for consent, data portability, the right to be forgotten, and mandatory breach notification. Its extraterritorial reach — applying to any organisation processing EU residents' data — made it the most consequential data privacy regulation globally. It forced companies worldwide to overhaul their data practices and inspired similar legislation in Brazil (LGPD), California (CCPA), and numerous other jurisdictions.
key_figures: Jan Philipp Albrecht (MEP, key GDPR negotiator)

sources.name: Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council (Official Journal of the European Union)
European Commission official guidance and implementation documentation

events.certainty_note: Official EU legislation with documented enforcement date.

events.title: Thai cave rescue (Tham Luang)
events.description: Between 23 June and 10 July 2018, twelve members of the Wild Boars youth football team and their coach were trapped in the flooded Tham Luang Nang Non cave in Chiang Rai province, Thailand. An international rescue operation involving Thai Navy SEALs, British cave divers, and specialists from multiple countries captivated global attention. The boys were successfully extracted through a complex diving operation over three days (8–10 July), though former Thai Navy SEAL Saman Kunan died during preparatory operations. The rescue was a rare unifying global news story and demonstrated international cooperation in crisis response.
key_figures: Saman Kunan (Thai Navy SEAL diver, died during rescue)

sources.name: Thai government press briefings and Royal Thai Navy official communications (June–July 2018)
BBC, CNN, Reuters, AP live coverage and subsequent long-form reporting

events.certainty_note: Global live media coverage with extensive documentation.

events.title: Russia presidential election and Skripal poisoning
events.description: Two significant Russia-related events shaped 2018. On 4 March, former Russian intelligence officer Sergei Skripal and his daughter Yulia were poisoned with the Novichok nerve agent in Salisbury, England. The UK government attributed the attack to Russian military intelligence (GRU), leading to the largest coordinated expulsion of Russian diplomats in history — over 150 expelled by 28 countries. Russia denied involvement. Separately, Vladimir Putin won re-election on 18 March with 77% of the vote in an election that lacked genuine competition. Together, these events deepened Russia's international isolation and reinforced the adversarial dynamics that would culminate in the 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
key_figures: Theresa May (Prime Minister of the UK)

sources.name: UK Government statement to Parliament by PM Theresa May (12 March 2018)
Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) technical assessment
Bellingcat open-source investigation identifying GRU suspects (September 2018)

events.certainty_note: Poisoning confirmed by OPCW analysis; Russian state responsibility established by multiple Western intelligence agencies and open-source investigation, though Russia officially denies involvement.

events.title: Yemen humanitarian crisis deepens amid Saudi-led offensive on Hodeidah
events.description: The Saudi-led coalition launched a major military offensive against the Houthi-held port city of Hodeidah in June 2018, threatening the entry point for approximately 70% of Yemen's imports and humanitarian aid. The United Nations described Yemen as the world's worst humanitarian crisis, with 22 million people requiring assistance, widespread famine conditions, and the largest cholera outbreak in modern history. International pressure eventually led to the Stockholm Agreement in December 2018, establishing a fragile ceasefire around Hodeidah, though the broader conflict continued. The crisis highlighted the devastating consequences of the proxy war between Saudi Arabia and Iran.
key_figures: Mohammed bin Salman (Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia)
Martin Griffiths (UN Special Envoy for Yemen)

sources.name: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) Yemen situation reports (2018)
Stockholm Agreement (December 2018)
Médecins Sans Frontières field reports; WHO cholera tracking data

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by UN agencies, NGOs, and international media, though access restrictions limited some reporting from conflict zones.

events.title: Pakistan elects Imran Khan as Prime Minister
events.description: Imran Khan's Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI) party won Pakistan's general election on 25 July 2018, and Khan was sworn in as Prime Minister on 18 August. The former cricket star ran on an anti-corruption, anti-dynasty platform promising a 'Naya Pakistan' (New Pakistan). Opposition parties alleged military interference and pre-poll rigging, and the election was preceded by the imprisonment of former PM Nawaz Sharif on corruption charges. Khan's victory represented a shift away from the two-party dominance of the PML-N and PPP that had characterised Pakistani democracy, though critics argued the military establishment had facilitated his rise.

sources.name: Election Commission of Pakistan official results
Dawn, Geo News, The News International contemporary reporting
EU Election Observation Mission Pakistan 2018 final report

events.certainty_note: Official election results, though the fairness of the process was disputed by opposition parties and some international observers.

events.title: Mexico elects Andrés Manuel López Obrador
events.description: Andrés Manuel López Obrador (AMLO) won Mexico's presidential election on 1 July 2018 with approximately 53% of the vote — the largest margin in modern Mexican history. His MORENA party also won majorities in both chambers of Congress. AMLO campaigned on combating corruption, reducing inequality, and pursuing a non-confrontational security strategy against drug cartels. His victory represented a leftward shift after decades of PRI and PAN dominance and reflected deep public frustration with insecurity, corruption, and economic stagnation. He took office on 1 December 2018.
key_figures: Andrés Manuel López Obrador (President-elect of Mexico)

sources.name: Instituto Nacional Electoral (INE) official results
El Universal, La Jornada, Reforma, Reuters contemporary coverage

events.certainty_note: Official election results with comprehensive documentation.

events.title: March for Our Lives and US gun control debate
events.description: On 14 February 2018, a mass shooting at Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School in Parkland, Florida killed 17 people. Surviving students rapidly organised a national movement for gun control, culminating in the March for Our Lives on 24 March in Washington, D.C., with an estimated 200,000–800,000 participants and hundreds of sibling marches globally. The student-led movement, featuring activists like Emma González and David Hogg, represented a generational shift in gun control advocacy. Florida subsequently passed the Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School Public Safety Act, raising the minimum age for firearm purchases to 21 and implementing red flag laws, though federal legislation stalled.

sources.name: Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School Public Safety Commission report
Florida Senate Bill 7026 (Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School Public Safety Act)
New York Times, Washington Post, Miami Herald, AP contemporary reporting

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented; crowd size estimates vary by source but the event's scale and political significance are not disputed.

events.title: Malaysia's historic election upset ousts ruling coalition
events.description: On 9 May 2018, Malaysia's opposition coalition Pakatan Harapan (Alliance of Hope) won the general election, defeating the Barisan Nasional coalition that had governed since independence in 1957. The 92-year-old Mahathir Mohamad became Prime Minister for the second time, having led the opposition against his former protégé Najib Razak. The election was driven largely by public outrage over the 1MDB corruption scandal, in which billions of dollars were allegedly misappropriated from a state investment fund. Najib was subsequently arrested and charged with corruption. The peaceful transfer of power was widely celebrated as a landmark for Malaysian democracy.
key_figures: Anwar Ibrahim (opposition leader, later pardoned and released)

sources.name: Election Commission of Malaysia official results
The Star, Malaysiakini, New Straits Times, Reuters contemporary reporting
US Department of Justice civil forfeiture complaints related to 1MDB (filed 2016–2018)

events.certainty_note: Official election results with full documentation; 1MDB allegations supported by multiple international investigations.

events.title: Rohingya crisis and ICJ accountability proceedings begin
events.description: The aftermath of Myanmar's 2017 military operations against the Rohingya continued to dominate in 2018, with over 700,000 Rohingya refugees sheltering in camps in Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh. In August 2018, a UN Independent International Fact-Finding Mission published its report concluding that Myanmar's military had committed genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes against the Rohingya, and recommended that senior generals including Commander-in-Chief Min Aung Hlaing be investigated and prosecuted. The report also criticised Aung San Suu Kyi for failing to use her moral authority to prevent the atrocities. The International Criminal Court authorised a preliminary examination in September.
key_figures: Min Aung Hlaing (Commander-in-Chief, Myanmar Armed Forces)
Aung San Suu Kyi (State Counsellor of Myanmar)

sources.name: UN Human Rights Council, Report of the Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar (A/HRC/39/64, September 2018)
International Criminal Court, Pre-Trial Chamber I ruling on jurisdiction (September 2018)
Médecins Sans Frontières mortality surveys; UNHCR refugee registration data

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by UN investigators, NGOs, satellite imagery, and survivor testimony.

events.title: Ireland votes to repeal abortion ban
events.description: On 25 May 2018, Ireland held a referendum on the Thirty-sixth Amendment to the Constitution, voting 66.4% to 33.6% to repeal the Eighth Amendment, which had effectively banned abortion since 1983. The decisive margin surprised many observers given Ireland's historically conservative Catholic social policies. The Health (Regulation of Termination of Pregnancy) Act was subsequently enacted in December 2018, permitting abortion up to 12 weeks of pregnancy. The referendum was seen as a milestone in Ireland's social transformation and the declining influence of the Catholic Church on Irish public policy.
key_figures: Savita Halappanavar (whose 2012 death galvanised the repeal movement)

sources.name: Referendum Commission of Ireland official results
Irish Times, RTÉ, The Journal contemporary coverage

events.certainty_note: Official referendum result with complete documentation.

events.title: Greta Thunberg begins school climate strikes
events.description: In August 2018, 15-year-old Greta Thunberg began a solo protest outside the Swedish parliament, skipping school every Friday under the banner 'Skolstrejk för klimatet' (School Strike for Climate). Her protest, amplified by social media, rapidly inspired the Fridays for Future movement, with students in countries worldwide joining weekly strikes by the end of 2018. Thunberg's activism connected to the urgency communicated by the IPCC SR15 report and represented a generational shift in climate politics, positioning youth climate activism as a major political force heading into 2019.

sources.name: Thunberg's social media posts and press interviews (August–December 2018)
Dagens Nyheter, The Guardian, BBC contemporary reporting

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in real time through media and social media.

events.title: US midterm elections: Democratic House wave
events.description: In the 6 November 2018 midterm elections, the Democratic Party won control of the US House of Representatives, gaining 40 seats — the largest Democratic gain since the post-Watergate 1974 elections. Republicans expanded their Senate majority by two seats. The elections saw historically high midterm turnout of approximately 50%, driven by polarisation around the Trump presidency. The results were notable for the election of a record number of women and the most racially diverse Congress in US history, including the first Muslim women (Rashida Tlaib, Ilhan Omar) and the youngest woman ever elected (Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, age 29). The Democratic House majority enabled investigations into the Trump administration.
key_figures: Nancy Pelosi (incoming Speaker of the House)
Donald Trump (President of the United States)

sources.name: Federal Election Commission official results; state election board certifications
US Election Project turnout analysis; AP, NYT, Washington Post election coverage

events.certainty_note: Official certified election results.

events.title: France's Yellow Vests (Gilets Jaunes) protests
events.description: Beginning on 17 November 2018, the Gilets Jaunes movement erupted across France, initially protesting a planned fuel tax increase but rapidly expanding into a broader movement against economic inequality, high cost of living, and perceived elite indifference. Protests involved road blockades, roundabout occupations, and weekly Saturday demonstrations in Paris and other cities, some marked by significant violence and property destruction. The movement, notable for its decentralised, social-media-driven organisation and lack of formal leadership, forced President Macron to cancel the fuel tax and announce €10 billion in emergency economic concessions in December. The protests continued into 2019 and influenced populist movements across Europe.

sources.name: French Ministry of the Interior protest participation figures and incident reports
Le Monde, Libération, Le Figaro, France 24 contemporary reporting

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through media coverage, government reports, and social media.

events.title: SpaceX launches Falcon Heavy
events.description: On 6 February 2018, SpaceX successfully launched its Falcon Heavy rocket from Kennedy Space Center, making it the most powerful operational rocket in the world. The launch included the successful simultaneous landing of two of the three first-stage boosters. The payload was Elon Musk's personal Tesla Roadster with a mannequin 'Starman' in the driver's seat, placed into a heliocentric orbit. While partly a publicity stunt, the Falcon Heavy demonstrated heavy-lift capability at significantly lower cost than legacy providers, advancing the commercial space industry and SpaceX's credibility for future contracts including national security payloads.

sources.name: SpaceX mission data and webcast (6 February 2018)
NASA Kennedy Space Center launch records; Spaceflight Now, Ars Technica coverage

events.certainty_note: Live-broadcast event with telemetry data and extensive documentation.

events.title: Armenian Velvet Revolution
events.description: In April–May 2018, mass nonviolent protests in Armenia led to the resignation of Prime Minister Serzh Sargsyan and a peaceful transfer of power. Sargsyan had attempted to circumvent term limits by shifting power from the presidency to the prime ministership. Opposition leader Nikol Pashinyan led weeks of civil disobedience, marches, and strikes that paralysed the country. He became Prime Minister on 8 May and called snap elections in December, which his My Step Alliance won with 70% of the vote. The 'Velvet Revolution' was celebrated as a rare successful democratic revolution in the post-Soviet space, though Armenia would face severe challenges with the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh war.

sources.name: Central Electoral Commission of Armenia election results (December 2018)
Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty Armenian Service, Civilnet, Hetq contemporary reporting

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented peaceful revolution with official election results.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed carry interpretive complexity that should be noted. (1) The Inter-Korean summits (2018-001) were widely framed as a 'breakthrough,' but some analysts argued Kim Jong-un's diplomatic opening was a strategic pause to consolidate nuclear gains rather than a genuine move toward denuclearisation — the absence of any concrete verification framework supports this interpretation. (2) The significance of the Trump-Kim summit (2018-002) is contested: critics argue it gave North Korea international legitimacy while receiving nothing verifiable in return, and the unilateral suspension of military exercises caught US allies off-guard. (3) Ethiopia's reforms under Abiy Ahmed (2018-006) were widely celebrated, but contemporaneous reporting noted that ethnic violence actually increased in 2018, with over 1 million internally displaced — the largest internal displacement globally that year — complicating the 'reformer' narrative that later earned him the Nobel Prize. (4) The 'democratic breakthrough' framing of Malaysia's 2018 election (2018-017) has been complicated by subsequent events: the Pakatan Harapan coalition collapsed by 2020, and Mahathir himself was criticised for slow reform. (5) Crowd size estimates for the March for Our Lives (2018-016) varied dramatically, from 200,000 to 800,000, and its long-term legislative impact at the federal level was minimal until 2022.
historiographic_note: 2018 is exceptionally well-documented through digital media, official records, international press, social media, and satellite imagery. The primary historiographic challenges are not evidentiary but interpretive: the sheer volume of information makes selection and significance-ranking inherently editorial. Coverage is skewed toward English-language media, events involving major powers, and dramatic political developments. Ongoing conflicts (Yemen, Syria, DRC, South Sudan) received less sustained attention than their humanitarian severity warranted. African, Central Asian, and Pacific Island developments are underrepresented in global English-language sources relative to their significance. Social media as a primary source presents archival and verification challenges that future historians will need to navigate.

graph_edges.note: Inter-Korean détente created the conditions for the Trump-Kim summit
2018 tariff escalation continued into broader trade war negotiations in 2019
Xi's power consolidation and US-China trade war reflect deepening great-power competition
Abiy's reforms destabilised TPLF power, contributing to the Tigray War
IPCC SR15 urgency galvanised Thunberg's school strike movement
Cambridge Analytica scandal reinforced political momentum for GDPR enforcement
Skripal poisoning and diplomatic expulsions were precursors to the broader Russia-West confrontation culminating in 2022
2017 Myanmar military operations produced the refugee crisis and accountability proceedings documented in 2018
Pashinyan government's foreign policy shifts contributed to dynamics preceding the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh war
Cambridge Analytica data harvesting was used in 2016 campaigns; exposed in 2018
Paris Agreement targets motivated the IPCC SR15 special report on 1.5°C
